commentBody: More magicians use a CAnE than a CAPE.

I found that very touph, and had to revisit most areas of the diaphragm over and over to finally get the last letter right. Seems there were a lot of clues endingh in the last character in this sentence — weren't there? But also a lot of nicely deceptible clues to boot.
I laughed out loud when I got VACANT LOT. Really enjoyed this Friday puzzle. Not much resistance but a fun solve.

I thought this was a nice Friday with tons of fresh entries and some off kilter clues.  The SE really slowed me down.  I had wand and then cane before CAPE, and tidal wave before ROGUEWAVE. Couple those missteps with not knowing of the MODELT or TELEGU and I was hot MESS for a hot minute.  I loved APECOSTUME and VACANTLOT.

So, in the Thursday puzzle, I had never heard of IHEARTRADIO. Wouldn’t you know, I was reclining in the dentist’s chair undergoing a root canal and an ad for IHEARTRADIO came on the tv playing in the room.
I was sure that my crosses were correct but I had no idea about telugu .needle drop and smash cut were new to me but inferable

“Careful what you wish for…” maybe I was tired and that one aggravated me at the end. I had CAnE instead of CAPE. I never heard of TELUGU. For some reason, I knew I could’ve stared at it for days and I wasn’t going to figure it out. I didn’t know what SEAGRAPES were. I’m not complaining. I haven’t had to look something up in a long, long time. It’s good for me.

Thank you to the constructors.

Tonight’s fun wrong guess: “Like some kitchens”= CLEAN As for the APE COSTUME: if there’s still anyone who hasn’t seen this - well I guess I’ve spoiled it. I was totally taken in the first time I saw it. https://youtu.be/vJG698U2Mvo

A Telugu association (they show movies and do things that help people keep the language going) sponsors one of those clean-up-the-highway things in Cary, North Carolina. I've driven past the sign hundreds of times. It was a delight to find a use for that word stuck in my head.

Fun puzzle that offered resistance but didn't frustrate me.

Nice Friday. It seemed about average difficulty to me at first, but then the SE definitely was a stretch for me especially since I was one of the folks who had WAND before CAPE and TIDAL for the WAVE. Very enjoyable over all and I also appreciated VACANT LOTS.
A few fun notes:
CLEAVE is a great word that can mean its opposite: to hold on to or cut in two.
Great shout out to FRED McMurray who was outstanding in that movie along with Barbara Stanwyck. It was weird seeing it after seeing him in “My Three Sons”.
Love the retro clue of BANANASEAT.
Finally nice dual movie clues with SMASHCUT and NEEDLEDROP, both of which were fairly new. What’s your favorite NEEDLEDROP? I suppose the quintessential one would be “Unchained Melody” in “Ghost” although I have a soft spot for “Somewhere Over the Rainbow” at the end of “You’ve Got Mail” and just about every song in “The Parent Trap” remake. What’s yours?

300 day streak.

Soooooo...

19A crossed with 6D. Two fictional characters, which means only one correct answer, but both S and Z in the crossing square yield common spellings of the respective names.

SEAGRAPES. I've had just about every type of caviar imaginable. Red, black, gold. Even lime and eggplant caviar. Never heard of green caviar.

It crosses with CAPE, which could be CAnE, BEGAT, which could be BEGoT, and TELUGU. So an esoteric word crossing with two squares that have alternate valid possibilities and an uncommon Proper Noun.

Took  *a lot* of trial and error to work it out, but I did in the end. 35 minute solve

Really enjoyed Maddy’s puzzle and Sean’s column. His discussion of constructor word lists was new to me, and I was fascinated by it. I may never be clever enough to construct a puzzle, but I definitely want my own word list! Thanks Maddy and Sean!

On a side note, I’ve been trying to restore a multi-year streak by appealing to the NYT Games crew. No response so far. (The app ended the streak despite solid gold star solves. It’s not the end of the world, but it’s wrong.) At the same time, I’ve been posting comments here since the error, but most of them don’t show up. Is it something I said?

Sheeple.

Both the word and the instances of the word scare the living daylights out of me.

I thought 39A [Sired] might be POPPED. 

But no.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Well...today's backhanded compliment is: there have been easier Fridays.
But I feel like the meal abruptly ended after the appetizer of SEAGRAPES.
To the archives, Robin!
(See now I've made myself all sad cause I was trying to reference the old TV show and I wrote "Robin" which naturally got me thinking about Friday "Robyn" and how she doesnt do puzles for us here anymore...Apologies, me...)

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: I will add that "Here goes nothing!" for VACANTLOT was most excellent.
Raising hand for people stuck with a CAnE for too long. Favorite NEEDLEDROP: Ragnarok with Zeppelin's Immigrant Song. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2defet1tLNs

At first I was surprised at how fast I was tackling the upper half of the grid, and even more surprised that I'd been able to break the puzzle open in the NW, something I rarely do on Fridays and Saturdays. Ah, but then came the lower half and that stubborn SE that I had to wrestle with. Glorious resistance and a few TIL opportunities to boot. Also loved the subtle misdirect of GMC being clued as [Sierra, e.g.] which had me thinking of the NATO alphabet and wanting the entry to be "ess." All in all, it was a very satisfying puzzle that SEGUEd straight to this also satisfying NEEDLE DROP: https://youtu.be/mng_tvLBH7Y?si=QjcwXfyTXuLciqPt Maddy, welcome back and thank you for this sparkler!

Sometimes I daydream about a small area of land. It's a lot to think about.

(I've got a one-tract mind.)

I found this impossibly difficult, and thus personally unenjoyable, as I'm not a fan of impossible challenges - take a look what I had after my first across and down pass, and what the grid ended up looking like: https://imgur.com/a/SKToc0g There simply was too much I didn't know, and I mean both proper names (TELUGU? SEA GRAPES? The name of the mother of a literary character I last thought about 20 years ago?) and expressions, and the clues just didn't vibe of with me. Much of my confusion was about cultural differences of course. Bikes with BANANA SEATs were never a thing here, and our difficult time for farmers is "przednówek" (literally, "[the time] before the new [harvest]"), that is late winter and early spring, when last year's harvest has been used up and one still has to wait for new crops. We don't get dry and wet seasons (but of course, with climate change, we can get long, devastatingly dry periods at any time: this spring has been the dryest in 70 years). Also, Matt and others below praise "Here goes nothing!" for VACANT LOT, and I don't get it, at all. My simple mind sees it like this: a vacant lot is not nothing - I own one and it certainly is something - and it's going nowhere, it just sits there. Clearly I'm missing something.

Ran into trouble all over the map. The gimmies were few and very far between. The Northwest was the last to fall. Had one flyspeck, and I was finished within minutes of my average. It was very satisfying to be done.
Fifteenth of May, five days to Eliza Doolittle Day. Just you wait (H)enry (H)iggins

What a treat that was, not nasty at all, when a right word suddenly emerged, all by itself, (sired-sired-sired BEGAT! and hoods-hoods-some kind of neighborhoods—no! WOODS!), and the fills started to tumble in.  

A rich Friday, full of surprises.  Thank you, Maddy Ziegler, you made my night!

Perfect Friday difficulty for me - intimidating at first, but slowly & steadily came together. Also, after welcoming a baby a few months ago and breaking a years-long streak, the first time a new streak has made it out of the teens!

Like others, I had TIDAL wave instead of ROGUE. I also had:

SUNDERS instead of CLEAVES,  

SPEED CUT instead of SMASH CUT, and 

YOUR CHOICE instead of IT'S UP TO YOU.

A fun, tricky puzzle.

It only took me a quick glance at my finished puzzle to Z why I didn’t hear any music.

Otherwise, what a fantastic Friday!

ROUGE WAVE made my day. 

Floating in the ocean is about peak spiritual experience for me. And many (it’s a long list) of my favourite animals live there. And my least favourite animal (human) doesn’t.

Arsenal winning both the Prem & Champions League may rocket me to a new spiritual high tho.

🏆🏆❤️🤍

CASEY/READE and HOSEA/EAMES were perceived as ‘not quite fair’ by this member of the  international contingent. 

Otherwise a fine Friday. 

Thanks

I don’t know why BANANA SEAT came to me instantly. I never had one. I was obsessed with Pygmalion and My Fair Lady as a child, so of course ELIZA was a gimme. Dredging Nancy’s friend BESS from the mental file cabinet took a bit longer but I got there. 

And isn’t that what crosswords are all about? Keeping the old file drawers from rusting shut while creating new folders? Nice combo of the old (FRED), the new (TELUGU), and the clever (VACANT LOT) in this one. Along with a timely reminder: don’t be SHEEPLE!
The Midi - "BABYBUMPS"? Really? Must do better...
Why is the clue for 30D in quotation marks? It’s just a colloquialism.

The definition of a NEEDLEDROP is using any licensed commercial music (that is, neither custom composed for the film, nor from a production music library) in a film scene. It doesn’t need to be “iconic” to count.
My favorites were I'm all ears and ape costume. Ape costume made me smile when I saw it.
Doña Inés (with an S not a Z) is not don Juan’s mother, she’s his love interest. Like the rest of this puzzle, too cute
A recent NEEDLE DROP I loved: "Two of Us" in "Project Hail Mary." I sobbed, it was so perfect
Fun stuff. Very fresh.

A somewhat easier Friday than usual for me - although I had to look up quite a few.

I'm amazed that SEA GRAPES emerged from the crossers. Impossible otherwise. I know TELUGU from teaching children from India, it has over 100 million speakers.

Some less common word were nice: BALED, BELABORS (caught out for a while with the spelling again) WAN, CLEAVES.

I was stuck on daily 'bread' for so long, but I recognise that LOT often goes with VACANT, whatever it means....
Just came to say, I think this was a perfect Friday puzzle. Just the right balance between fun — at times, whimsical — and challenging.

Had TIDALWAVE and WAND crossing in SE corner.
Took a while to straighten that mess out.
Yet another fantastic puzzle.
I'm grateful for all of the NYT constructors who create these gems.
Too 'American' for this lowly foreigner...

That was a fun puzzle full of a lot of rewarding clues but also really hard even by Friday standards.

It was hard to get a real foothold anywhere.  But top half at least had a few easier ones like ENO and ISTO to get me to the crossers there APE COSTUME and BANANA SEAT.

The southeast corner though, holy crap.  TELUGU crossed with ROGUE WAVE and SEA GRAPES, which also crossed an archaic word RAN, also proper nouns like BESS which you need specific knowledge to know.

Then the magician clue they also crossed which wasn't hard in itself but had a few equally valid answers like Wand, Card, Deck, meaning I couldn't commit to an answer until those very hard crossings.
Was pretty tricky for me, but BANANASEAT,  ROGUEWAVE and CISTERN really opened up the right side of the puzzle for me.

Lovely puzzle. 

So much good.

I hate to join the cacophony,
but my solve time was 14 seconds 
longer than Tuesday.

And Tuesday was breezy and lovely.

Just sayin’.

Maybe I’m just grouchy.

But it was a lovely puzzle.

Oh, man, there's beauty in the box today:  LIVE WIRES, NEEDLE DROP, LET ME AT ‘EM, ROGUE WAVE, SEA GRAPES, SMASH CUT, APE COSTUME.  Those last five are NYT debut answers, by the way.

All these answers are longs – eight letters or more, of which today’s puzzle has a sky-high 16 – which have the potential to invigorate a puzzle, and they sure do today.

Then Maddy throws in a contronym – a word with two opposite definitions – with CLEAVES.  Language-quirk-loving me adores contronyms, such as sanction, ravel, splice, garnish, oversight.

The solve gave me areas of whoosh, and spots of struggle, both of which happify my brain.  And two TILs, both worth knowing: TELUGU and SEA GRAPES.

This is Maddy’s second Times puzzle.  After her first, also a themeless, I commented that this was a constructor with potential.  Today’s entry solidifies that suspicion, and Maddy, I hope they keep coming.  Thank you – I loved this!

Got ready to call "Natick!" on TELUGU/MODELT
Modelt...Modelt...Modelt...Huh???
Ohhhhhh MODEL T
I been dooked!
Per the banana/ape costume discussion: where I lived when banana seat bikes were popular, the high handlebars those bikes used were called "ape hangers."

It’s amazing to see all the different experiences we have with puzzles. For me, this one was super easy and I completed it 16 minutes faster than normal with no lookups! Probably a first for a Friday puzzle. But yesterday’s puzzle killed me while many of you loved it. Just goes to show that each puzzle is aimed at a particular set of knowledge and language. That’s why I love it!

Love those roe grapes found near the sea waves.

Yesterday I thought I saw a troop of gorillas downtown, but it was just a bunch of sheeple in apes’ clothing. On banana bikes.

A nice puzzle.

APECOSTUME…

… sure dude, whatever

The four corners were not too difficult, but the center vexed me for a bit. Got everything in place without assistance, but took almost half an hour.

"Here goes nothing!" was a clever clue for VACANTLOT, which misdirected me for a while. Having LEMME AT EM rather than LET ME AT EM also caught me, though clearly MATT was needed for the crossing entry. Didn't know SMASHCUT or NEEDLEDROP, but both were easy enough to discern.

Solid Friday puzzle, but to me not one of the STUNNERS.

I started the puzzle in the NW, not the usual place for me on a Friday, and thought, wow, this is ridiculously easy. That continued for the top half. Then I got my comeuppance.

Two film clues that I never would have gotten in a million years. TELUGU. SEA GRAPES. And a few others. Potential double Natick in the SW, but as a New Yorker and regular solver, I knew Duane READE, and of course, CASEY. But GMC took a while.

In the SE, I struggled a little. But I remembered Bess, having owned the entire set of Nancy Drew books. 

In the end, I managed to complete the puzzle without any lookups, but unlike most others, I found it a slog.

What saved me in the SE was that I had the "pleasure" of being a victim of a ROGUE WAVE. I was on a cruise returning to NY across a very rough North Atlantic. I was a veteran cruiser, but this cruise line was a new company for us. The incompetent captain docked us in the ocean for 24 hrs. to avoid a storm.Then he drove right into the storm. I was sitting at the desk next to the balcony door on Deck 11, yes eleven!, when a ROGUE WAVE came crashing onto that door. It lifted me and my chair and hurled me halfway across the room, where I landed in the small space between the foot of the bed and the wall, with my legs still wrapped around that chair. I was bruised but unbroken. I went to the main desk to report the incident, passing people who were bleeding. The crew couldn't have cared less. Never cruised with Norwegian again.

Well, typical tough Friday workout for me. More than a couple of things that were never going to dawn on me from the clues, but just a lot of working the crosses until things fell together. And... puzzle find today. A Sunday from October 12, 2014 by Pawel Fludzinski with the title: "Inner workings." Thought this one was pretty clever. Some theme answers: COALCANARYMINE ROUNDSQUAREPEGHOLE MILLIONNOTYEARS CANDYKIDSTORE TEATEMPESTPOT HANDGOTOHELLBASKET HAYNEEDLESTACK Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/12/2014&g=84&d=A ....
Great puzzle, great Friday. Another grid that was really hard but finally got it done, 7D was the last to fall.

A fine Friday puzzle. Only a few gimmes and lots of freshness. I especially liked NEEDLE DROP and SMASH CUT. Tricky, but gettable. We've been asked to give our favorite NEEDLE DROP, and the first that comes to mind is the powerful "Feeling Good" by Nina Simone in Wim Wenders' exquisite "Perfect Days". https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oHRNrgDIJfo&list=RDoHRNrgDIJfo&start_radio=1 That's my favorite in this movie, but the entire soundtrack is magnificent!

What with BELABORS and BEGAT, not to mention the MODEL T, the WASHTUB, and the BACKWOODS, one suspects a time warp!  But anyone who calls a BANANA SEAT a "vintage feature" isn't looking back very far.

I call Foul on 3D!  If you're going to end with AT 'EM, then you start with LEMME, not the dainty LET ME.  

TiL about the 2000+ year-old language TELUGU, spoken by an estimated 100 million people. ...can't wait to drop this into a pertinent conversation...or is there a form that could be the Wordle of the day?..I suspect the Editor(s) of allowing this in the puzzle just to create an occasion for upbraiding our ethnocentric thinking,, etc.

I think @Francis has already mentioned that the word SHEEPLE gives him the willies. I understand that. The word always reminds me of the wonderful (and awful!) New Yorker cartoon from the Deal Maker in Chief's first run for office. "He tells it like it is." https://condenaststore.com/featured/he-tells-it-like-it-is-paul-noth.html?srsltid=AfmBOopmqq8SucSYYPNccqBOycASlUsCAQ4xtxf25dfg2V-jdjZ82fxr
Thanks for the link in the Column for the viperfish...

I don't usually start with a long answer, especially on Friday and Saturday, when the constructor is often trying to be tricky and surprising.  But today's first entry for me was BANANA SEAT, and it immediately triggered a wave of nostalgia for me.

My brother had a bike with high handle bars and a BANANA SEAT in the early '70s.  It was a Huffy, I think, and everyone called it a StingRay (like the Corvettes of the era).  He was the only one in the neighborhood to have one, and it certified his image as the coolest dude on the block.
He was younger than me, so I couldn't admit that I was jealous.  Instead I had to find some way to torture him.  

Ah, the joys of sibling rivalry!

When I was around 6 years old in the early 70s, my father got a bike for me at a garage sale. It was an amazing gold-colored Schwinn Sting-Ray with a banana seat and those high curved handlebars (we might have called them monkey bars). It was absolutely the coolest bike ever!  Then my annoyingly sensible father replaced the banana seat and handle bars with standard versions, making it one of the lamest bikes ever. I was devastated 😭😭😭
Today is Brian Eno’s 78th birthday.
Took me a minute to realize what a Modelt was...

A pleasant tussle.  Not so hard as to drive me crazy, but plenty of thinking required.  For me the hardest answer was the language:  I had CAnE for the magician's accessory -- which would have left me with SEA CRANES/TELUcU as the cross.  But SEA CRANES did not sound like a Thing -- certainly not something you would eat with great pleasure like caviar.  What to do?

Aha!  If it's a CAPE for the magician clue, then I can get to SEA GRAPES/TELUGU.  And while I've never heard of SEA GRAPES, I can picture them looking like caviar, if not exactly tasting like caviar.  As for the Indian language: You say TELUGU and I say TELUCU -- let's call the whole thing off.

And that was just one section!

Re SMASH CUT -- Don't know what it is, but it sounds much too "jarring" for me.  Sounds like a movie I should miss -- and I plan to.

Other new to me answers: BANANA SEAT and NEEDLE DROP.

Question:  Is there such a thing as WASHTUB ABS?

Lively and interesting, this puzzle felt very fresh to me.

Having spent 4 months in southern India, where the dominant language is Tamil, I was perplexed by 41A. Tamil didn't fit!  Had to look up Telugu on a map (after getting it from the crosses). I guess that's southern India but it looks more central to me.... It made me curious what people from India consider "southern." After all, the "South" in the US is really the southeast.
Excellent puzzle Maddy – thanks for the pleasant start to the day.

"Sticks" had me going for a while, especially as the PGA is in my neck of the woods this weekend. @Marshall is probably dealing with some heavy traffic. Then I got WOODS filled in, which only reinforced my fixation on golf. Laughed out loud when I finally got it.

Still not happy with "Here goes nothing," but I see that's been thoroughly argued about already. I guess it "goes" on the market.
Fun!

Got hung up on the spelling of READE (I had an extra E giving me a name of CESEY which I shrugged and let slide), and then TELUGU crossing SEAGRAPES. My fill of CANE for 54A left me trying to think of possible names for "green caviar," and it turns out I don't have strong feelings on that topic and again shrugged and moved on. I fill in the last square and get no joy, and so the review began!

Speaking of Sid Sivakumar, I’m bummed that we haven’t had a puzzle from him in 18 months. He’s one of my favorite constructors.

ROGUE WAVE brings to mind the heartbreaking final scene from “The Perfect Storm”. Great movie. 

A favorite NEEDLE DROP is Don’t Stop Believin’ from the last episode of The Sopranos.  Steve Perry initially withheld his permission to use the song until he was assured by the producers the final scene would not be violent. 

A nice start to the weekend.

Took several minutes of poking around to figure out what I'd done wrong before I gave up at looked at the key. Turns out I had ELIsA and INEs instead of ELIZA and INEZ. That's a really dreadful cross.

My best Friday time ever. Really enjoyed solving this one, and thought the "vacant lot" clue was genius. I also thought the forum moderator missed disapproving a certain much-responded-to post. Differences of opinion, likes, dislikes--great. Crude to the nth and worse that gratuitously unpleasant? Spare us, please. And, really folks, if you simply ignore the whiners and attack dogs, their posts won't piggyback to the top of reader picks on your responses where they then dominate what otherwise can be a very worthwhile conversation. There are some very smart, knowledgeable, and creative people here who make the day more interesting, and I say, let 'em prevail.
Speed fill on the NE corner! Did not prepare me in any way for the rest, which was (for me) a classic Friday level. Thanks for the fun!
Is the constructor the same Maddie Ziegler who's an actress/dancer? I noticed the name is spelled differently. Great puzzle!

For those who don’t like VACANTLOT—sure this is vague, tangential, clever but ungettable without crosses—after all, how many myriad things could have nothing here? But that’s exactly what makes it a great weekend clue. Again I have to take you back. March 26th 2016. Try it and tell me that puzzles haven’t gotten easier and it’s just me being more experienced. Not even my favorite puzzle, by any means, but full of tangential clues like this that push you to your limit. I had scads of white space on this puzzle for ages. Just forced myself to take breaks, jump around, maybe guess some likely letters (often wrong), and just meditate for a few minutes. Lots of words and terms I didn’t know, or barely. Common words clued in ways I didn’t recognize. Absolutely thrilled and proud to solve it without lookups, and if finally I would have had to, so be it.
Don’t get me wrong, this was a worthy weekend puzzle today, and we’ve had a lot of them lately. And maybe many of you would not be disappointed by the demise of puzzles like the one I referenced. Just don’t call us crazy for imagining that the difficulty level has changed, and when you see a clue like this realize that once this was the norm not the outlier.

I'm not a big Tom Cruise fan, but even so, his star turn in his tighty whities has to be in the running for the greatest NEEDLE DROP of all time. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y_gcV7Nmeaw Give me that Old Time Rock & Roll any day, Bob Seger!
"monkeysUit" just happens to be the same length as APECOSTUME. Very sneaky!
Not the easiest puzzle and VACANTLOT was really only peeve I'd have clue wise.  Still, Gold Star and bested my average by 15:30, no complaints!
You’re officially old when your bike as a kid is vintage.
Loved this one. Are SEA GRAPES still a thing? Seemed impenetrable at first, yet after a few misdirects, I finished up at 21:20. That is very good for me as I am not a fast solver, nor do I aim to be.
Struggled mightily with this one, especially the N/NE sections. Its good to be humbled once in a while

[Competitor of the early Chevrolet 490] meant nothing to me, so I asked my autophile* Partner--
"Six letters, first letter M . . ."
"Well, the 490 was a high-horsepower muscle car, so, MUSTANG?"
"Too long."
"How about MACH I (which was basically a souped-up Mustang)" 
"Too short--maybe MACH II?"**

 . . .

"Looks like we're both wrong, by about four decades--works out to MODEL T!" 

Per wikipedia, the Chevrolet 490 was so dubbed as its price, when introduced in 1915, was $490.00--which represented a five dollar savings over the base-model Model T, as $495.00

*"autophile* means "lover of automobiles," and not "lover of one's self," as might be expected.  He's a Sweetie, with a selfless disposition.

**The Ford Mach 2 (so styled) never made it past a prototype and into production.

As usual lately, I haven't had a chance to read comments yet, but I just wanted to say once I removed wand from 54 across, I quickly learned how very many words start with CA that could possibly be a magician's accessory. You've got your cane, your card, your cage... I think they were a few others that I tried before CAPE. Hahaha! SEAGRAPES never occurred to me because in Honduras, where I'll be already at this time next week, there are little fruit that grow along the shoreline that they call seagrapes. My husband loves them!! The golf cart comes to a screeching halt anytime he sees them. Just kidding, we're never going very fast out there. Anyhow, here is a little information about them for anyone who might be interested. https://mahindranursery.com/blogs/plant-guide/coccoloba-uvifera Fun puzzle! I enjoyed it, though that lower right and right middle area were my toughest spots so I added to the hard rating on x word stats. But all very doable in the end with some persistence and digital erasing of things. All those CA words, lemmeatem before let me, REeDE before correcting it. Etc etc. Too many to mention. Anyhow, I am off to a taco lunch with a friend now! Hopefully I'll have a chance to read some comments later. Cheers to the weekend!

Found this slightly more difficult than recent Fridays, but it did end in a more satisfying finish (once I finally got there).

Loved most of the long answers in this: LIVE WIRES, LET ME AT EM, NEEDLE DROP, BANANA SEAT and APE COSTUME were all very fun (I particularly enjoyed entering BANANA above APE). 

Wish I could say the same for SEA GRAPES ("green caviar" is slightly out of my tax bracket, so I've never heard of the variety). The SE corner in general was, for me, the bane of this solve. I began with BEGOT, not BEGAT, and was unfamiliar with TELUGU. For "magician's accessory" I trial and errored CANE, CAGE and even HARE (when one runs out of white rabbits, naturally) before finally landing on CAPE and picturing Liberacci and Dracula magic shows. 

MODEL T had me thinking MODEST and FOREST before realising it was a split answer. I also didn't see DOES until I had OES, and was imagining something a tad less pedestrian for "pulls off". 

VACANT LOT and NIT were my final entries. I am blissfully unaware when it comes to basketball competitions, and I couldn't get VACAY out of my head for "here goes nothing!", but I had no idea how that would pair with LOT. I think my reasoning was blanking out a calendar for personal days? 

Mostly enjoyable, however, and I always feel more accomplished solving grids with an above-average count of long entries. Bravo Maddy!
Very brutal puzzle for me that I had to auto check and soon reveal. But: VACANTLOT got a good laugh out of me

I've been trying to figure out how to weave this into a post--a brilliant cover of a well-loved(?) song with lyric connections to the puzzle--was it ever used as a NEEDLE DROP? Not that I've seen-- anyway: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9QK1TlkT5iI&list=RD9QK1TlkT5iI&start_radio=1 (Hi, @Grant!)

OK, FOAT NEEDLE DROPS--
1) Season 1 finale of Six Feet Under--Blue Öyster Cult's "Don't Fear the Reaper."
2) Season 3 finale of Queer as Folk--New Order's "True Faith"
(3) Iris Dement's "Our Town" in the pastiche at the end of the final episode of Northern Exposure--might not count.)

I live up against the Pacifica Ocean. If you ever see those signs warning about "Rogue Waves", take them seriously. They only install them after a tragedy, not before. Happens several times a year, and each story is heartbreaking. Respect the ocean, beachcombers!
Hands up if any one else thought yesterday’s was a lot tougher than today’s? Somehow I was on the same wavelength as the constructor. Made for a satisfying birthday solve!

Phew! I only had ESL, ISTO and ENO on first pass and got a little panicked. It slowly started coming together, but until the very end I was sure I'd be running the alphabet to hear the music given how much I didn't know. But no! Turned out to be one of those neat puzzles where almost everything was gettable or guessable with a few crosses, and the few really unguessable answers had solid enough crosses to avoid the sad alphabet run finish I was anticipating. A nice Friday puzzle with some fresh fill.

Fun puzzle -- I thought it was going to be too easy when I zipped through the north, but I am grateful that I got bogged down in the south. 

SEA GRAPES! I'm familiar with this species -- I have worked on a group of nudibranchs (sea slugs) that have a very specialized diet -- the saccoglossan slugs. They have a radula (basically, a tongue with teeth on it) that is specialized to slit open large cells. A few of them eat only fish eggs, specializing in one or a few species of fish. Since eggs are just huge cells, one puncture and you can suck up the entire innards of the egg. A great reward for little work. 

But most saccoglossans slit open coenocytic or siphonaceous algae -- filamentous algae that don't have cell walls separating their "cells" They do undergo mitosis, producing two nuclei where there was one, but instead of forming a new cell by laying down new cell wall between them they just elongate and do it all over again -- the result is a long, multinucleate filament. So when a saccoglossan slits open the cell wall of a coenocytic alga, the entire length of the filament is just there to be sucked out. "I drink your milkshake!" 

I did not study the species that preys on SEA GRAPES but a related species that preys on algae found in New England.

The fun part of why I studied these is that the slugs digest everything except the choloroplasts. They incorporate the chloroplasts into their own cells and use them to photosynthesize. Photosynthetic animals!

2 comments: I wish the NYT would not allow spoilers in the comments, like they don’t allow them in the spelling bee. I also wish that the new editors would be more thoughtful about some of the clueing. Today’s colon clue is like many that I’ve seen recently that I feel are unsolvable, and therefore makes the puzzle not fun. On the other hand, I do see that there is a lot of original clueing that I love and I also love that there are more contemporary references and  that’s something that’s positive. I see that a lot of players love this puzzle so I maybe I’m the outlier.

@Cat Lady Margaret

This is a classically hilarious demonstration of human foibles.

There's another great example in which someone stops someone on the street to ask directions.  While the "mark" is giving the directions, a big object is carried between them, eclipsing the person who asked for directions for second or two.  When it's gone, the question-asker has replaced by someone else, and the person giving directions rarely notices.

I am incredibly unobservant.  You can ask me if someone wears glasses, or has a mustache or is bald, and I won't remember the face well enough to answer with any authority.

Probably why I am an ex-chemist.  I should have been a detective.

@Cat Lady Margaret 

I saw it, but had stopped counting by then.

@Cat Lady Margaret I heard the researcher Chabris talk about this experiment and the serious real world implications on witness statements. See https://nobaproject.com/modules/failures-of-awareness-the-case-of-inattentional-blindness for a good description of the work. The relevant passage: "Chabris and colleagues simulated a famous police misconduct case in which a Boston police officer was convicted of lying because he claimed not to have seen a brutal beating (Lehr, 2009). At the time, he had been chasing a murder suspect and ran right past the scene of a brutal assault. In Chabris’ simulation, subjects jogged behind an experimenter who ran right past a simulated fight scene. At night, 65 percent missed the fight scene. Even during broad daylight, 44 percent of observers jogged right passed it without noticing, lending some plausibility to the Boston cop’s story that he was telling the truth and never saw the beating."

@Daniel Lemke

In the criminal law class at Law School we were basically told that from a forensic science perspective, witness statements are essentially useless - already then, 25 years ago, there was much research to support this position.

@Cat Lady Margaret

I am very much with you there.  

I mean, everything we see, touch, imagine is something, if nothing more than a place for something else to be.

True nothingness is terrifying.

Dogs, for the most part.

Complete nothingness is only terrifying in one scenario - when you're in the middle of it. One of Stephen King's most terrifying short story, for me, was "The Jaunt", which explored the concept. The closest I have ever personally been to that was on my wife's nephew's 1st birthday party - for me those were five hours of absolute, life-destroying boredom, devoid of anything of any value or interest to me. It's been 10 years now and I still shudder when I think of it.

@Francis

I didn't say this was a difficult puzzle - I just described it as difficult for me. What's controversial here?

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

"Here goes nothing!" goes like this:

So let's say you're sitting in on a municipal planning committee and the members are brainstorming how to make best use of a land parcel.

"Over here goes a convenience store," says the comptroller.

"Oooh...and over there goes a dog park," says the mayor.

"And maybe over there goes the daycare center," says the landscaper.

"But what about that irregularly shaped vacant lot in the northeast pasrt of the parcel?" asks the commissioner.

Silence ensues for a few minutes as all struggle to figure out what to put there.

Finally, the building janitor, who's been listening while tidying, boldly approaches the group, points at the space and declares:  

"Here goes nothing!"

Then I went into a local grocery store and asked if they had a “BIC Banana”, which was a popular bright yellow writing pen in the early 70s. If I can solve the dictionary words, that's what makes it 'easier' and if the words are interesting or unusual - that makes it more enjoyable. 
Some terms are half-way to easy - like Mel Ott, RBI (thank you Lewis and co) and some are now officially easy - like Oreo, Ora, IHOP. 
Just as, if you were solving a British crossword and saw a clue with 'me old China', you might not know it referred to 'friend' - from China plate=mate in rhyming slang.

@Dave Munger It's kinda like if the different parts of the US spoke different languages instead of having different accents/dialects. In India it's like that, except it's also the official language of each state. There's the south, regionally and linguistically because those languages aren't actually related to the North Indian languages. You were in Tamil Nadu, so everyone spoke Tamil, but you can go about halfway up the country and still be in South India, and go through at least 4 other languages! As a Telugu speaker in America it made me happy to see ot in the puzzle :)

@Grant

@Anita You led me to go look up Sid Sivakumar. I hadn't remembered, but now I realize he's had some really amazing puzzles. One example - A Thursday from September 3, 2020. Don't recall another like this. Amazing theme answers. I'll put the circled letters in the answers in caps. The trick on each theme answers was to start at that circled letter and then bounce back from the end. A couple of examples: EKACNA(P)OTA (Potato pancake) NOTGNIHSA(W)ALLAW (Walla Walla Washington) DAER(B)ANA (Banana bread) Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=9/3/2020 ...
That was a good one, Rich!

@Captain Quahog so interesting.

In case anyone is interested, this is the species I studied. I collected them on Martha's Vineyard. You may have heard about the sea slug that can decapitate itself and then regrow its entire body. That has been shown in two species in the same genus (Elysia) but not in this one -- yet! https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elysia_chlorotica

@Captain Quahog  Really cool stuff, Cap. Have not seen a Nudibranch referred to in AGES.Brought back memories of my undergrad years at Cornell- spent 2 summer sessions at the Shoals Marine Lab (off Portsmouth NH) and invertebrate biology there was such a treat 🦪. All that remains is to work “veliger larva” into a conversation (sorry for the marine bio geek out folks).

@CatsPaws - I know a lot of people at Shoals from years past -- Mike Lesser, Win Watson, Megan Dethier (now the director at Friday Harbor) and so on. 

I've been out there for the end-of-season pig roast, which I hear hasn't happened in many years now. Too bad -- it was a great example of resource husbandry and conservation. Are you old enough to have experienced that?

@Maamazon

This comment section wouldn't exist if spoilers weren't allowed. There's really no way to talk about the puzzles without them. 

And it's okay to be an outlier. We don't all love every single puzzle, and like some more than others. 

I knew the colon clue immediately. But even if you didn't, I think the crosses were relatively gentle there, so hopefully you were able to figure it out. 

I don't think there's anything in any puzzle that everyone immediately knows off the top of their head, without some crossings etc. 

I'm glad you're seeing other things out here like the more contemporary references and original cluing that you're enjoying. I don't know how long you've been solving, but keep at it and even things like the colon will come to you quicker. I know they have for me in the just few little years I've been solving.

@Maamazon I’ve never seen the colon clue before, and got it fairly quickly. Though I can’t say the same for a lot of other clues, this was a hard one for me today!! But that was my personal experience, and I don’t consider the puzzle unsolvable just because I found certain parts so difficult.
